title: The Death of Caesar
description: Senators led by Brutus and Cassius assassinate Julius Caesar in the Senate house.

latin_text.text: Iūlius Caesar, dictātor Rōmānus, postrīdiē Īdūs Mārtias in senātum intrāvit.
senātōrēs coniūrātī, ubi eum vīdērunt, togās suās aperiēbant ut tēla celāta
ēdūcerent. Caesare vīsō, Brūtus et Cassius eum circumstetērunt.
Caesar rogāvit cūr senātōrēs tam prope stārent. subitō gladiīs petītus est.
trēs et vīgintī vulnera accēpit. Brūtō etiam percutiēnte, Caesar gemuit et
togam suam super vultum trāxit nē moriens spectārētur.
corpus eius ad basim statuae Pompeiī cecidit. multī senātōrēs attonitī fugiēbant;
aliqui crēdēbant rem publicam servātam esse.

vocabulary_notes.meaning: on the day after the Ides of March (15th March)
dictator (title of absolute power at Rome)
of Pompey (genitive — former rival of Caesar)

model_translation.text: Julius Caesar, the Roman dictator, entered the Senate on the day after the Ides of March. The conspiratorial senators, when they saw him, were opening their togas in order to draw out hidden weapons. When Caesar had been seen, Brutus and Cassius surrounded him. Caesar asked why the senators were standing so close. Suddenly he was attacked with swords.

He received twenty-three wounds. When even Brutus struck him, Caesar groaned and drew his toga over his face so that he would not be watched as he died. His body fell at the base of Pompey's statue. Many senators were fleeing in astonishment; some believed that the state had been saved.

key_phrases: entered the Senate on the day after the Ides of March
opening their togas in order to draw out hidden weapons
why the senators were standing so close
drew his toga over his face so that he would not be watched as he died
fell at the base of Pompey's statue
some believed that the state had been saved
